name: AWS WAF JA3/TLS Fingerprint Allowlist Monitoring Pack
summary: Monitors high-sensitivity endpoints for clients whose TLS/JA3 fingerprint context does not match a trusted allowlist, starting in COUNT mode to safely baseline and tune before any selective BLOCK enforcement.
objective: Harden sensitive application surfaces against automation and spoofed clients by using TLS fingerprint context as a low-risk, production-first anti-bot signal.
risk_mitigated: Credential stuffing and scripted abuse using non-standard client TLS stacks
Low-effort bot traffic targeting login, account recovery, and payment flows
Client impersonation attempts that diverge from known app/browser network fingerprints
assumptions: AWS WAF logging is enabled and monitored via CloudWatch Logs, Kinesis Firehose, or S3/Athena.
High-sensitivity endpoints are identifiable by URI path patterns and/or host headers.
JA3/TLS fingerprint context may be available via AWS WAF labels/request context depending on integration and traffic path.
Teams can tolerate an observation period before enforcement.
prerequisites: AWS WAF v2 WebACL already exists and is attached to CloudFront or ALB.
Security operations workflow in place for reviewing WAF COUNT events.
Known-good client population (web, mobile, SDK) has been sampled to build an initial trusted fingerprint list.

rules.description: On sensitive endpoints, count requests where JA3/TLS fingerprint context is present and not in trusted allowlist.
rules.statement_logic: IF request matches sensitive endpoint scope AND JA3/TLS fingerprint signal exists AND fingerprint NOT IN trusted allowlist THEN COUNT

rules.description: Separately count requests to sensitive endpoints when JA3/TLS fingerprint context is absent, to avoid blind blocking and support fallback tuning.
rules.statement_logic: IF request matches sensitive endpoint scope AND JA3/TLS fingerprint signal is absent THEN COUNT

tuning_notes: Start with a narrow endpoint scope (for example: /login, /account/recovery, /checkout) before expanding.
Mobile and app client churn can be high across OS/app/library updates; expect JA3 drift and refresh trusted fingerprints regularly.
Do not treat JA3 as a standalone identity control. Combine with rate limits, auth telemetry, IP reputation, and device/app attestation where available.
Create separate allowlist buckets per channel (browser, iOS, Android, backend integrations) to reduce operational noise.
Use COUNT metrics to identify top non-allowlisted fingerprints by endpoint and successful-auth ratio before considering any BLOCK action.
Maintain an emergency override path (label bypass/IP set/scope-down exception) for critical client regressions.

fallback_behavior.when_fingerprint_context_absent: Do not block solely due to missing JA3/TLS context. Keep in COUNT and route through existing baseline protections (managed bot control, rate-based rules, IP reputation, auth anomaly detection).
recommended_control_path: Managed rule groups and bot controls
Credential abuse detection on auth outcomes

deployment_notes: Phase 1 (COUNT): Deploy both rules in COUNT mode with sampled logs and dashboards for at least 7-14 days.
Phase 2 (Observe): Validate top fingerprints by endpoint, map to known client populations, and update allowlists for legitimate churn.
Phase 3 (Selective BLOCK): Convert only the non-allowlisted-sensitive rule to BLOCK for narrowly scoped endpoints with stable client fingerprints; keep context-absent rule in COUNT.
Phase 4 (Iterate): Reassess after app releases, SDK upgrades, and TLS stack changes; maintain a recurring allowlist review cadence.

kpis: Auth success/failure ratio for counted requests
Top JA3/TLS fingerprints by request volume
False-positive candidate rate from support/security tickets
alerts: Sudden spike in non-allowlisted counts on login endpoints
Persistent growth of context-absent counts suggesting telemetry/path regression
Post-enforcement increase in failed legitimate authentication attempts

terraform_snippet.description: Example wiring into an existing aws_wafv2_web_acl using COUNT-first rule and a ByteMatchSet/IPSet placeholder for trusted fingerprints or label-driven matching.

limitations: JA3/TLS fingerprint availability and exact matching mechanism can vary by AWS service path and integration details.
Fingerprints can be spoofed and should not be used as a sole trust decision.
Aggressive blocking without staged observation may impact legitimate mobile/app users due to TLS stack churn.
